findings.description: `recoverBootGoroutine` silently swallows panics in boot goroutines. The Tasker's stated justification ("a leaked Add(1) would deadlock the drain forever") is incorrect — Go runs deferred functions during panic propagation, so `wg.Done()` and `registry.done()` would execute even without the `recover()`. The real effect of `recover()` here is a behavior change: before this ticket a panic in `runFrsIdleSweeper` / `runWelcomeEmailConsumer` would crash the pod and trigger a clean k8s restart; after this ticket the same panic is logged and the worker silently stops while the pod keeps serving traffic. In a financial system, a silently-stopped FRS sweeper (the very worker this ticket was designed to protect) is a worse failure mode than a crash, because the "stamp DB + publish" path simply ceases and dashboards still show a healthy pod.
findings.fix: Either (a) drop the `recover()` and let panics crash the pod — defers will still run so the WG is not at risk — or (b) keep `recover()` but re-panic after running the WG/registry defers, or (c) keep `recover()` AND add a `bay_session_boot_goroutine_panic_total{goroutine=<name>}` counter so SRE has a paging surface beyond log search. Update the doc-comment to remove the inaccurate WG-deadlock reasoning regardless.

findings.description: `waitForShutdownDrain` unconditionally bumps `CascadeRefundShutdownAbandonedTotal{phase="wait_timeout"}` on any drain timeout, including timeouts where only named boot goroutines were in flight and zero cascade refunds were active. Existing alerts paired with the refund-recovery worker (which scans for un-stamped smg_bet rows) will fire spurious tickets when only boot goroutines stalled. The Tasker documents this as "operationally acceptable" because the recovery worker's scan is a no-op, but a noisy false-positive alert is still operationally costly at 3am.
findings.fix: Only bump the cascade counter when the WG had cascade-refund work in flight. The cleanest path: split the WaitGroup back into two (`detachedWG` for cascade refunds, `bootWG` for named workers) and drain them in parallel via a single `time.After` so the cumulative budget is still bounded. Alternatively, add a `cascadeInFlight atomic.Int64` to track active cascade refunds and only bump the legacy counter when it is non-zero.

findings.description: `cleanup()` falls back to `defaultGoroutineDrainTimeout` (25s) when `cfg.GoroutineDrainTimeout <= 0`, bypassing the `< ShutdownTimeout` invariant that `validateCoreFields` enforces only when the value is non-zero. A test fixture that constructs `Config{ShutdownTimeout: 10*time.Second}` directly will end up with a 25s drain budget inside a 10s shutdown — the property the validation was designed to prevent.
findings.fix: Either tighten validation to reject zero (force every caller to set it explicitly), or have the cleanup-time fallback clamp to `min(defaultGoroutineDrainTimeout, ShutdownTimeout - someBuffer)`. The Tasker's stated reason for tolerating zero is test-fixture ergonomics — a small helper `defaultDrainBudget(cfg)` would solve that without the silent overrun risk.

findings.description: `parsePositiveSecondsEnvVar` does `time.Duration(secs) * time.Second` with no upper bound on `secs`. On 64-bit hosts an attacker (or a fat-fingered operator) setting `BAY_SESSION_GOROUTINE_DRAIN_TIMEOUT_SECONDS=10000000000` overflows `time.Duration` (int64 nanoseconds, ~292 years max) and yields a negative or wrapped value.
findings.fix: Add an upper bound (e.g. `secs <= 86400` — a one-day cap is more than enough for any sensible drain) and return a wrapped error above it.

reviewers.notes: The mechanism is sound: shared WG covering cascade refunds + named boot workers, bounded drain with per-name attribution, validation that the drain budget fits inside `ShutdownTimeout`. `TestSigtermMidFrsSweep_PublishLandsBeforeCleanup` is honest about being a mechanism test (worker doesn't observe ctx); the assertion `publishTS < closeTS` is the right behavioural shape and would survive a from-scratch rewrite of `waitForShutdownDrain`. The panic-recovery point is the only one I'd push back on hard — the stated rationale is technically wrong and the resulting failure mode is non-obvious. None of the findings block ship; the panic-recovery concern is worth a follow-up ticket before this lands in production.

findings.description: `validateCoreFields` skips the validation check `c.GoroutineDrainTimeout >= c.ShutdownTimeout` when `c.GoroutineDrainTimeout == 0` (to support ergonomic test fixtures). However, in `buildDeps`'s `cleanup()`, a zero value is replaced with `defaultGoroutineDrainTimeout` (25s) before being used. If `Config{}` is instantiated directly with a zero `GoroutineDrainTimeout` and a short `ShutdownTimeout` (e.g., 10s), the fallback causes the drain budget to silently exceed the overall shutdown timeout. This causes a timeout inversion where the orchestrator forcefully kills the process before the graceful drain can complete.
findings.fix: Resolve the default fallback value *before* asserting the timeout invariant in `validateCoreFields`. For example: `effDrain := c.GoroutineDrainTimeout; if effDrain <= 0 { effDrain = defaultGoroutineDrainTimeout }; if effDrain >= c.ShutdownTimeout { return fmt.Errorf(...) }`.

findings.description: `recoverBootGoroutine` catches and swallows panics from boot workers, allowing the goroutine to exit cleanly. The comment claims this prevents a drain deadlock, but standard `defer` statements (like `wg.Done()`) execute during panic unwinding regardless, so the `WaitGroup` would not leak. By swallowing the panic without signaling the application to shut down, the pod becomes a "zombie" — it continues to pass HTTP liveness probes indefinitely while critical background loops (like `frs_idle_sweeper`) are permanently dead.
findings.fix: Do not silently swallow panics. Either re-panic at the end of `recoverBootGoroutine` (e.g., `panic(r)`) to crash the process, or trigger a graceful process shutdown by invoking a global context cancellation when a critical boot worker crashes.

reviewers.notes: The shutdown join mechanism and its testing strategy are otherwise very clean and well-structured. Correcting the Config validation inversion and addressing the zombie-pod panic behavior will bring this to reference quality.

findings.description: `launchBootGoroutine` now wraps every boot worker in `recoverBootGoroutine`, and `recoverBootGoroutine` swallows the panic after logging it. In Go, an unhandled panic in any goroutine normally crashes the process; this change silently converts fatal worker crashes into permanent background-feature loss. If `runFrsIdleSweeper`, `runWelcomeEmailConsumer`, or the stream-stats scraper panic once, the pod stays up but that worker is dead forever until an external restart. In this system that can mean idle-session cleanup stops, welcome-email messages stop draining, or observability quietly degrades without the self-healing restart behavior the service previously had.
findings.fix: Do not swallow boot-worker panics just to preserve `WaitGroup` bookkeeping. Either remove the recovery entirely, or recover only long enough to emit the log and then terminate the process explicitly so the orchestrator restarts the pod. If you need both clean `wg.Done()` behavior and crash semantics, recover in the wrapper, log, call `wg.Done()`/`registry.done()`, then re-panic or `os.Exit(1)` from a fatal path.

findings.description: The new “integration” coverage does not exercise the actual FRS sweeper, actual `cleanup()`, or an actual publisher close path; it only proves that a synthetic goroutine blocked behind a channel can hold a `WaitGroup` open. That is useful unit coverage for the drain helper, but it does not verify the ticket’s highest-risk production path: a real `runFrsIdleSweeper` iteration performing store mutation followed by publish while shutdown is in progress. A regression in the real sweeper call graph could still ship with this test green.
findings.fix: Add a true integration-style test around the real shutdown path: start the actual sweeper with controllable store/publisher doubles or a real test fixture, trigger cancellation mid-iteration, invoke the real cleanup closure, and assert the publish completes before the publisher/client is closed. Keep the current mechanism test if desired, but it should not be the only acceptance coverage for this ticket.
